preFix.title: Sterilising and reusing glass jars for food storage and preserving
preFix.subtitle: Which jars are suitable for reuse, how to sterilise them correctly, and the distinction between storage and preserving
preFix.excerpt: Glass jars from jam, pickles, and condiments are reusable for dry storage, fermentation, and in many cases fresh preserving. The key is knowing which lid types are safe for repeated use, how to sterilise correctly, and the difference between clean-enough for a dry store and safe for a preserving process. A jar that looks clean may still harbour the bacteria relevant to food safety.
preFix.sourceNotes: National Centre for Home Food Preservation (NCHFP) guidance on jar reuse and sterilisation. Soil Association jam-making and preserving guidance. Guild of Food Writers fermentation and preservation guides. RHS guidance on storing and preserving harvests.

content.text: Most commercial glass jars are made from the same glass as dedicated preserving jars. The difference is in the lid seal. Dedicated preserving jars (Kilner, Le Parfait, Mason, Ball) use replaceable rubber seals and metal clips or two-part lids designed for repeated sealing under pressure. Commercial jam jars have a one-piece twist-off lid with a plastisol sealing compound that degrades over repeated heat cycles. This distinction determines what each type can safely be used for.

Kilner bail-top or two-part screw-band jar: designed for repeated full preserving cycles. Replace the rubber seal annually or when it shows any cracking. The glass body lasts indefinitely if unchipped.

Commercial jam jar (twist-off lid): fine for dry storage, fridge leftovers, fermentation, and fridge pickles. Not reliable for a boiling-water-bath process. The seal may not close again after heating.
Jar with a metal lid and any visible rust: discard the lid. The rust indicates the sealing compound is compromised. Replace with a new lid of the correct diameter.
Wash jars in hot soapy water and rinse. Place right-side up on a clean oven shelf.
Heat oven to 140 degrees C. Once at temperature, place jars in the oven for 15 minutes.
Remove carefully and fill while still warm (a cold jar receiving hot jam can crack; a warm jar does not).
Do not oven-sterilise lids with rubber seals or plastisol compounds. The oven degrades both. Sterilise lids separately in boiling water for 5 minutes, then dry on a clean rack without wiping.

A dishwasher on its hottest cycle (usually 65-70 degrees C) sterilises jars well enough for dry storage and most cold-process preserves. It does not reliably reach 100 degrees C, so use oven or boiling-water methods for full-process preserves.
Dry goods: pulses, grains, spices, dried fruit. Oven or dishwasher sterilisation adequate. Lids need only be clean, not sterile, for dry goods.

Fermentation crocks: sauerkraut, kimchi, lacto-pickles. No sterilisation needed; the fermentation process kills pathogens. Clean jars with hot water only, as soap residues can inhibit the starter culture.
Refrigerator preserves: pickles and jams stored in the fridge rather than on the shelf. Commercial twist-off jars are fine. Shelf life of 4-8 weeks once opened.
Full shelf-stable preserving: use dedicated preserving jars with fresh rubber seals and follow the full boiling-water-bath or pressure-canning process. Commercial jars are not reliable for this.

attrs.fix: Use this jar immediately (within 2-3 days) from the fridge rather than storing on the shelf. Discard any jar processed with a commercial lid that did not seal after a heat process.
attrs.cause: The jar has not achieved a vacuum seal, either because the lid was already used and the sealing compound had set, or the jar was underfilled
attrs.symptom: Lid button has not popped down after a boiling-water-bath process
